original_abstract:
Finding the maximum independent set (MIS) is a fundamental NP-hard problem with extensive real-world applications. In recent years, branch-and-reduce algorithms—which integrate reduction rules into a branch-and-bound framework—have become the state-of-the-art for solving MIS exactly, particularly on large-scale, practical instances. While significant progress has been driven by the discovery of increasingly powerful reduction rules, other critical algorithmic components have remained relatively under-explored. One such component is the branching strategy, which dictates the order in which vertices are processed. Although selecting the vertex of the highest degree has long been the standard heuristic, its performance is often suboptimal.

In this work, we introduce a novel branching strategy guided by Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Due to the structural complexity of modern solvers, traditional supervised and reinforcement learning techniques are difficult to implement in this context. To overcome this, we employ a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the model’s parameters directly toward faster execution times. Our experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of this learning-based approach, achieving a speedup on 73% of benchmark instances with a median improvement of 24%.
humanized_abstract:
Given its numerous applications, the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem remains a widely studied NP-hard problem. In the recent years, branch-and-reduce algorithms have become the fastest exact algorithms on real-world instances. The design of efficient reduction rules has played a crucial role in recent advances, while other components have been somewhat neglected. In particular, while selecting a vertex of high degree has been the default branching strategy for decades, there is little understanding of the performance of this choice, and there is no clear argument to support it.

We present a new approach by employing a branching strategy steered by a Graph Neural Network (GNN). Due to the inherent intricacy of the state space of today’s solver implementations, it is generally not feasible to apply either supervised or reinforcement learning. We therefore make use of a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the model’s weights towards small values, which are associated with higher solver performance. Experimental results on a set of challenging benchmark instances confirm the feasibility of learning-based solver technologies. The speedup achieved on 73% of our benchmarks ranges from a factor of 2.4 up to 25.4 with a median value of 24.

document.input:
Finding the maximum independent set (MIS) is a fundamental NP-hard problem with extensive real-world applications. In recent years, branch-and-reduce algorithms—which integrate reduction rules into a branch-and-bound framework—have become the state-of-the-art for solving MIS exactly, particularly on large-scale, practical instances. While significant progress has been driven by the discovery of increasingly powerful reduction rules, other critical algorithmic components have remained relatively under-explored. One such component is the branching strategy, which dictates the order in which vertices are processed. Although selecting the vertex of the highest degree has long been the standard heuristic, its performance is often suboptimal.

In this work, we introduce a novel branching strategy guided by Graph Neural Networks (GNNs).
